Risks & Conclusion

While I continue to think SPYD is a solid fund, there's a likelihood it will continue to underperform vs other popular dividend-focused ETFs.

Their high exposure to Real Estate is likely a turn-off with their distributions considered ordinary income.

They are also at higher risk of underperformance should we see volatility in long-term treasuries once base rates decline. However, I do consider them a solid fund for anyone looking to prioritize income and who already has a diversified portfolio.

With a yield above 4% and solid total returns over the past decade, State Street's S&P High Dividend ETF may be a fund to consider going forward.

data.title: IGR: NAV Erosion Likely To Continue Unless Dividends Are Reduced
data.description:
Overview

When I previously covered the CBRE Global Real Estate Income Fund (IGR), I issued a hold rating due to the questionable dividend coverage and uncertainty around interest rates. While the rest of the market sells off, real estate has seen a bit of a revival in the last few months. Additionally, IGR has released an updated annual report for the 2025 period, which prompted me to reassess the fund's value proposition, performance, and outlook for 2026.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that IGR's share price has declined by nearly 7.2%. However, the fund has experienced some positive momentum over the last two months. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 7.5% over the same time frame. IGR now offers investors a starting dividend yield of 14.6% while issuing payouts on a monthly basis. However, I believe that the generous distributions are harmful to the overall sustainability of the fund. IGR would benefit from a distribution cut because it would help improve the NAV growth.

The recent rise in share price has resulted in IGR now trading at the higher end of its price-to-NAV range. Therefore, valuation is a bit tricky at this time since the underlying earnings haven't improved year-over-year. Although interest rate cuts may be a catalyst at some point, I don't think this is the best time for long-term investors to accumulate shares. So let's start by taking a look at the strategy that IGR implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund overview, IGR has total managed assets of $1B that are spread across 81 different holdings. The fund's primary objective is to provide a high level of current income from a global range of real estate securities. However, the fund is unique because it combines traditional common stocks with preferred stock to achieve a blend of capital appreciation and income generation over time.

The fund is most heavily allocated to U.S.-based securities while offering meaningful exposure to global securities across different regions of the globe. Approximately 60.7% of assets are focused on the U.S., with the remaining portion of its portfolio being spread across regions of Europe, Asia, and Australia.

Expanding on this diversity, IGR maintains an efficient allocation across the different sub-sectors of real estate. Therefore, IGR can avoid any sort of concentration risk to specific focus areas. As we can see below, IGR is most heavily allocated to retail REITs at 14.70% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to industrial and healthcare securities, which accounts for 14.03% and 13.18% of assets, respectively.

When looking at the top positions of the fund, we can see many notable names. The fund is also slightly top-heavy since the top ten positions make up more than a third of total assets. Welltower (WELL) is now the largest holding at 6.25%. This is followed by an allocation to many other notable names, including Equinix (EQIX), American Tower (AMT), Simon Property Group (SPG), and Crown Castle (CCI), just to name a few.

The fund also utilizes leverage as a way to amplify its earnings when possible. The use of leverage currently amounts to 31.82% of assets, which is equivalent to ~$300M. I consider this to be an aggressive use of leverage in this high interest rate environment because leverage can amplify risks. For instance, leverage can amplify losses if allocated to securities that decline in value. Since interest rates are elevated, the cost of debt may be negatively impacting the fund's operating spread. Despite several cuts throughout 2025, interest rates still remain near the higher end of their ten-year range.

Updated Valuation & Performance Insights

When I previously covered IGR, the fund traded at a discount to NAV of 1.5%. Following the small price rally over the last month, IGR now trades at a premium to NAV of 2.08%. For reference, IGR has traded at an average discount to NAV of 4.94% over the last five years. Referring to the red line on the graph below, IGR now trades at the higher end of its historical price-to-NAV range. Therefore, I don't believe this is the best time to accumulate shares for long-term investors.

Since my last coverage, IGR has released an updated annual report for the 2025 period. This is the most up-to-date report that provides us with transparency into the fund's earnings and financial highlights. Net investment income amounted to $0.09 per share, while net realized gains landed at $0.03 per share. Looking back on prior years, IGR's net investment income levels have been low ever since interest rates were raised to their decade highs. This could imply that the fund's operating expenses are heavily impacting the operating spread for IGR. Additionally, the low investment income means that IGR is heavily dependent on net realized gains to make up for this lower income.

The only challenge is that net realized gains have also been suppressed and wildly varied in this current interest rate environment. So despite market indices racing to all-time highs during 2025, IGR's net realized gains of only $0.03 per share weren't quite a reflection of the overall market strength. This variability makes it very hard to predict how the fund will perform during any given period. Furthermore, this reliance on net realized gains can negatively impact the trajectory of the NAV over time. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2021: $10.48 per share
  • 2022: $6.31 per share
  • 2023: $6.20 per share
  • 2024: $5.10 per share
  • 2025: $4.50 per share

As we can see, the fund's NAV has continued to decline year over year, which is a clear indication that something is wrong. Since the fund continues to pay out more than it actually earns, the underlying NAV and share price will continue to erode over time. When looking at the long-term performance of the fund, we can see that IGR's share price has declined by more than 34%. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 81.6% over the same time frame. So while investors should anticipate that most of their returns will come from the distributions that are paid out to shareholders, this is an instance where the distribution may actually be harmful to the long-term success of the fund.

Furthermore, the focus on income generation may translate to underperformance over a longer holding period, especially if those distributions aren't supported by earnings. A good example of this can be demonstrated when comparing IGR's performance against a more traditional sector-tracking ETF, such as the State Street Real Estate Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLRE). Over the last ten years, XLRE has provided a total return of 102.8%, which outperforms IGR over the same time frame. Since XLRE doesn't prioritize income generation, its positions are capable of growing over time without disruption, whereas IGR has to sell positions in order to support distributions.

Dividend Risks

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.06 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 14.6%. If we take this monthly distribution and multiply it by twelve months, we get an estimated annual payout of $0.72 per share. Referring back to the same annual report for 2025, it is clear that IGR struggles to generate the necessary earnings to support the distributions that are being paid. However, IGR decided to raise dividends back in 2022 and keep these payouts elevated. Although the fund isn't able to support these distributions, I believe this was strategically done as a way to shrink the fund's discount to NAV valuation.

Now that IGR trades in the premium-to-NAV territory, I anticipate that a healthy distribution cut may need to be implemented as a way to bring the fund back in alignment with success. A large distribution cut of 50% would still provide investors with a competitive dividend yield of ~7.3% and would relieve the fund's pressure to produce higher earnings. A lower payout would increase the probability that IGR will produce excess cash flow that can help change the trajectory of the NAV momentum. If IGR decides not to reduce the distributions, the NAV will continue to erode over time and negatively impact returns for shareholders.

Lastly, we can get confirmation of IGR's generous distribution policy by referring to the latest Section 19(a) notice. This confirms that 91% of the most recent distribution was classified as return of capital, which means that IGR has not generated enough earnings to support distributions, so they are being funded directly from the NAV. While these distributions may be more tax-efficient, they can be harmful for the fund and cause erosion when heavily depended upon for a long stretch of time.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my hold rating on IGR at this time. The fund provides you with direct exposure to some of the highest quality REITs in the world, but the underlying structure is inefficient. The fund's earnings have remained suppressed in this high interest rate environment, so the fund has struggled to generate earnings that can actually support payouts. As long as IGR pays out more than it earns, the underlying NAV will continue to erode over time. This will limit shareholder returns compared to more standard sector-tracking ETFs like XLRE. Therefore, I do not believe this is an appropriate time to accumulate just yet.

data.title: Meta: Time To Sit On The Fence (Downgrade)
data.description:
Meta Platforms, Inc.’s (META) share price has rallied notably since my previous bullish call until late January, when the stock suffered a post-earnings selloff due to the panic related to aggressively increasing AI-related CAPEX. Thus, META currently trades almost at exactly the same price as it did in mid-December.

As a Meta investor, I am also quite concerned about the company’s plans to aggressively ramp up investments in data centers. The company’s Q4 earnings were stellar, but I think that with a significant increase in capital investments, Meta’s historically strong ROIC is at risk. The management sees potential to improve value for digital advertising clients by adding new AI-powered features for improved targeting, but the competition in generative AI models has become so intense, and Meta doesn’t look like the most powerful one in this domain. Meta's digital advertising business is already quite mature, and it is difficult to expect skyrocketing profitability from the legacy segment because of its scale and likely increasing competition for marketing budgets with LLMs. All in all, I am not buying more META shares and believe that it is a hold at the moment.

Recent developments

Meta released its latest quarterly earnings on January 28th, delivering another dual beat against consensus estimates. Revenue growth was solid during the quarter with a 23.8% YoY increase, while the adjusted EPS expanded from $8.02 to $8.88. It was a quality EPS expansion, as it was underpinned by almost a 10% increase in EBITDA, which reflects improved operating efficiency.

While Q4 performance was extremely strong with big positive surprises in terms of both revenue and EPS, there is an elephant in the room, which is the management’s initiative to significantly increase capital investments in FY2026. Meta’s annual CAPEX already reached almost $70 billion in FY2025, which is almost a five-fold increase compared to FY2020. And even this huge annual CAPEX of $70 billion last year is far from peak, because the management said during the earnings call that they expect FY2026 CAPEX to be between $115 billion and $135 billion. The midpoint is $125 billion, which is a 79% increase compared to FY2025 and a 3.4x increase compared to FY2024.

One of the reasons why I have been investing in Meta for several years is because of the company’s extremely high average return on capital employed [ROIC] of almost 23% over the last decade. The difference between return on invested capital and cost of capital is pure shareholders’ value, and Meta has been historically very strong in maintaining a wide window between these two metrics. With all that being said, Meta's profits must grow in line with its rapidly growing investments in fixed assets. Capital employed is total assets minus current liabilities, and we can see that this amount has increased significantly over the last few years.

The above table suggests that Meta's capital employed increased by 123% over the last five years. Given that the management plans to increase investments in CAPEX by $125 billion in FY2026, it will be quite reliable to say that capital employed will increase by at least $100 billion over the next year. In relative terms, it will be approximately a 30% increase in capital employed. Therefore, to prevent ROIC compression, Meta's operating profit should also demonstrate the same growth in FY2026. I am saying "operating profit" instead of NOPAT [net operating profit after tax] for the purposes of this article for simplicity purposes.

I think that such an increase in the operating profit is highly unlikely because Wall Street analysts expect Meta's EPS to be approximately flat in 2026 compared to 2025. Therefore, ROIC compression looks inevitable by the end of FY2026 at least.

Moreover, there is a notable fundamental risk for Meta that the global digital advertising market can be disrupted by generative AI companies. The global digital advertising market is huge and rapidly growing, expected to hit almost $2 trillion over the next decade.

Generative AI companies like OpenAI (OPENAI) and Anthropic (ANTHRO) are also investing hundreds of billions in compute power, and expanding into the digital advertising market looks like an extremely reasonable step considering the large user bases for their flagship LLMs. For example, Sam Altman said a few weeks ago that his company might replicate Instagram-style ads in ChatGPT. Besides these two giant pure-gen AI plays, there is also Google (GOOG) (GOOGL). It is a real monster, which is already the global digital advertising leader, rapidly developing its own generative AI model, Gemini.

Of course, Meta is also developing its own LLMs, but it is behind the aforementioned players in terms of market share. Therefore, there is an increasing risk of Meta losing the race for marketing budgets of clients to generative AI models.

Valuation update

Meta is down by approximately 1% YTD, meaning that its market cap remains at the $1.6 trillion level. Meta looks perfectly fairly valued at the moment, as its current multiples are very close to historical averages across the board. Moreover, the PEG ratio of 1.08 is almost identical to the sector median. Therefore, based on valuation ratios, it appears that there is not much upside left on the table after the share price has sextupled since late 2022 lows.

If we take the consensus EPS forecast for FY2026 of $30.24 and multiply it by the historical forward non-GAAP P/E ratio of 22.7, we get the target price of $686.5. This is approximately 5% higher than the actual share price, which is a very limited upside potential. I think that using Wall Street’s EPS forecast for FY2026 is a reliable approach because Meta very rarely misses consensus earnings forecasts.

Risks to my rating downgrade

While my valuation update suggests that Meta is approximately fairly valued, Wall Street analysts disagree with me, as their consensus target price is $863. This means a solid 31.8% upside potential from current levels, which might add a lot of optimism to the market. Therefore, analysts price new big positive surprises or potential earnings revisions, which is always a possible scenario when we speak about a stellar company like Meta.

While I have doubts about surprises from growth potential generated by organic and in-house initiatives, there might be some moves from the M&A front. It is a possible scenario because Meta’s growth strategy is multi-faceted and the company makes strategic acquisitions sometimes. There is a proven record of extremely successful acquisitions [Instagram, WhatsApp], and the company’s balance sheet is rock-solid, with more than $80 billion in cash and extremely low debt relative to Meta’s $1.6 trillion market cap.

A potential acquisition of an already profitable company will highly likely add to positive long-term EPS revisions, which will inevitably result in positive repricing. On the other hand, over the short term the stock price usually dips after acquisition news or even rumors.

Bottom line

To conclude, I am downgrading Meta to a neutral Hold rating because I am also very concerned about the company’s ability to maintain its historically stellar ROIC after all these huge investments in AI infrastructure. Moreover, valuation analysis suggests that there is almost no upside left on the table.

data.title: Sabesp: A Successful Privatization Story, But Less Asymmetric Now
data.description:
In my last coverage of Sabesp (SBS), I reiterated a “Strong Buy” rating. In doing so, the Brazilian ADR has done very well, accumulating total returns in excess of 50%, counting dividends up to the time of writing.

In doing so, I recognized the bull case from the company being a newly privatized state-owned company in Brazil (in this case, in mid-2024). At the time, the state of São Paulo in Brazil owned 50.3% of the company, and post-privatization, the stake fell to 18.3%, and it was no longer the controlling shareholder.

When the company was state-owned, decisions tended to be slow and dependent on politics. After privatization, the expectation was for cost-cutting initiatives and more professional management. And this would eventually have an impact on margins and return on capital for shareholders.

Along with the privatization (where around R$14.7 billion was raised), came a giant investment plan, "CapEx", to commit R$70 billion to universalize water and sewage treatment—which still does not serve all families in Brazil's main state.

Each investment goes into the RAB “Regulated Asset Base,” which basically determines how much return the regulator allows the company to earn through tariffs. Of course, sanitation is a regulated sector. The allowed return (regulatory WACC) is usually around ~8% on the asset base.

So basically, the engine of Sabesp's thesis comes from the RAB amplified by the regulatory return yielding profits. I go over what has happened since my last take on the company—almost a year ago—to update why it's still worth being constructive on SBS even with the recent strong valuation.

Privatization Is Beginning To Show Operational Results

As expected, Sabesp's CapEx really accelerated post-privatization. The company reported R$10.4 billion in the first 9 months of 2025—up 151% vs. 9M24 and R$13.2 billion ttm. Investments in sewage grew by +240% and water by +46%, showing that the R$70 billion of CapEx planned has started to come to fruition.

This is reflected in the expansion of the customer base, with 948,000 units with sewage treatment, 733,000 new units with sewage, and 616,000 new units with water—with 148,000 new connections added in 3Q25 alone, the last quarter reported. This is proof that infrastructure expansion has generated physical system growth.

Perhaps more importantly, the long-awaited post-privatization efficiencies are already showing up in costs. OpEx fell 16% 9M25 vs. 9M24, a direct implication of the reduction in personnel (-12% YoY in 3Q25) and better collection of water and sewage bills.

And the icing on the cake is that even with the massive investment, adjusted EBITDA is rising. Sabesp posted in Q3 R$3.2 billion in Adj. EBITDA, up 14.7% YoY and an Adj. EBITDA margin of 59% vs. 51% in my period last year. Also, the balance sheet looks pretty solid with cash and financial investments at R$11.5 billion vs. R$3.5 billion in the same period last year, with total assets up to R$96 billion from R$76.1 billion—basically pure RAB growth.

The central idea is that with Sabesp able to report EBITDA close to R$12-14 billion annualized, it is possible to finance part of the CapEx with its own cash flow.

Revisiting Sabesp’s Value Creation Through RoRAB

It seems quite evident that the original thesis of investments accelerating efficiency is being well executed, and the strong performance of the ADR subscribes to that.

But again, in the midst of the ADR's strong rally, is this the time to take the plunge and realize gains?

In my latest coverage, I evaluated SBS value generation capacity through the lens of Return on Regulated Asset Base, i.e., “RoRAB”—which is basically the operating profit “EBIT” divided by the RAB. This metric is preferable due to the regulatory model that involves tariff issues and fixed regulatory returns. In April last year, I arrived at a RoRAB of 18.3% for Sabesp—about twice the regulatory WACC.

Now, one year later, the RAB is reaching roughly R$84.3 billion (R$95.9 billion in total assets minus R$4.9 billion in cash and R$6.6 billion in financial investments), and based on trailing EBIT of R$10.3 billion, I arrive at an updated RoRAB of about 12.3%. But again, keep in mind that water and sewage are a regulated sector in Brazil, and there is a regulatory WACC of ~8%. That being said, a RoRAB above this level still implies substantial value creation—just not as much above the regulatory return as it was in April last year.

Moving on to valuations, taking into account the R$130.35 billion of enterprise value that SBS trades at, this would equate to an EV/RAB of 1.55x—also above the 1.22x that served as a pillar for my "Strong Buy" rating issued in April last year. In other words, clearly the market has already recognized and reassessed the multiple of the regulated base.

Lastly, here's an update on the RAB projection based on a new base. The R$84 billion RAB, when considering this year's R$15 billion CapEx guidance, and over the next five years (2030) reaching R$75 billion, would result in a future RAB of ~R$160 billion, very close to my original model of R$167 billion, showing that the thesis seems to be well calibrated.

A Brief Word on Earnings

As Sabesp's earnings are just around the corner (the company is expected to post its fourth quarter results later on March 16th), consensus is working under expectations of a revenue decrease of 19% YoY and an EPS increase of +49% YoY. The explanation, however, for such seemingly weak top-line growth is due to 4Q25 coming from a distorted accounting comparison base and a mix between items recognized below the operating line vs. the revenue line—as the company noted in its 3Q25 earnings call.

On the EPS side, even if revenues come in weaker shape, tied either to the comparative base or accounting distortions, the bottom line should still come in strong given the improvement in adjusted EBITDA margin driven by post-privatization efficiency enhancements, especially regarding headcount reduction.

Rather than the top and bottom line numbers, investors will likely be looking for the CapEx trajectory and management's narrative towards its capacity to sustain the investment pace required to meet universalization targets. I believe that CapEx coming in at a range of R$4 billion to 4.5 billion in the fourth quarter—pretty much in line with the actual R$15 billion annual guidance—would represent that the original R$70 million investment plan through 2029 is moving accordingly, and ensure the steady expansion of the RAB, which ultimately supports future tariff adjustments and long-term growth. CapEx numbers below the yearly guidance would likely raise questions in terms of management's execution capacity and the attainment of universalization targets.

Risks And Final Thoughts

Sabesp's biggest risk is the regulatory landscape, especially since 2026 is an election year not only for the President, but also for Congress, the Senate, and the government of every state in Brazil. The implication of lower tariffs and possible political pressure to reduce water tariffs tends to impact revenue and return on RAB directly for the thesis. In December last year, Sabesp went through its first post-privatization tariff review, and the average water tariff was adjusted for inflation and came to ~R$6.7/m³—while in the state model it would have come to R$7.36 m³—which I consider a victory for the company.

That being said, I believe that although Sabesp's thesis has been going well, much of the original asymmetry has closed, and so I wouldn't expect 2026 to be a year of such robust returns as 2025 was. However, as Sabesp continues to report a RoRAB above the regulatory WACC, this still signals a very welcome generation of value.

For this reason, I remain constructive, although more aware of the asymmetry, believing that SBS remains a thesis to be pursued in the long term based on the clear improvement in efficiency post-privatization, which will tend to provide attractive returns to its shareholders both in terms of dividends and added value.

data.title: Goodyear Tire: Asset Sales Shift Gears To Earnings Growth
data.description:
Introduction

Goodyear Tire (GT) has spent 2025 reshaping its business as it works to improve profitability and reduce leverage. Through a series of asset sales, cost initiatives, and a focus on higher-margin tire segments, the company has been simplifying operations and strengthening its balance sheet. While the demand for tires is a bit uneven in the near term with a softer market, I think the controllables are changes that are beginning to show up in Goodyear’s margins and financial position. I’m of the view that these efforts should translate into earnings growth and a rerating in the stock’s valuation.

Goodyear has reduced leverage through divestitures

Goodyear has made a number of divestitures in 2025 in an effort to reduce leverage. Some of these include the sale of Off-the-Road Tires (OTR) to Yokohama Rubber in February last year for $905 million and the sale of the Dunlop brand to Sumitomo Rubber Industries (SRI) in May last year for $735 million. Soon after, the company sold Goodyear Chemical to Gemspring Capital for $650 million. Totaling $2.3 billion, these sales were used to deleverage the balance sheet and streamline the company to focus on its own core "Goodyear" and "Cooper" brands. By offloading non-core or lower-margin assets, Goodyear’s made progress on the balance sheet and improved profit margins.

While one might look at these divestitures as a negative since they reduce sales, I think that even though they aren't selling more "brands," Goodyear is continuing to rationalize its footprint. This includes closing high-cost facilities (like the mold facility in Findlay, Ohio, set for March 2026) and shifting production to lower-cost regions to save an additional $300 million this year. Some of the divestitures also made strategic sense, like the fact that Goodyear had shared the Dunlop brand with SRI for decades through various joint ventures meant selling the rights back to SRI just simplified the ownership.

FY'25 results showed progress on improving profitability

As for the latest quarterly results, Goodyear’s Q4 revenue came in at $4.9 billion, down 0.6% year over year (in line with sell-side estimates) due to a 3% decline in volumes. Most of this has been driven by weaker consumer replacement demand (with a 14% drop in North American commercial volumes) and because of the divestiture of the OTR and chemicals businesses. The offset to this was a 4% increase in revenue per tire and an 8% increase in consumer replacement pricing.

With lower volumes and higher pricing, Goodyear has made good progress, with Q4 being the consumer replacement pricing. Key highlights from the quarter include the strongest segment operating income and margin performance in over seven years. In terms of the underlying core business, the company noted a 30% record increase in new product launches focused on higher-margin segments with strong pricing actions in the U.S. and Canada in response to tariffs, meaningful consumer OE share gains. Alongside the three divestitures, the company has shown good execution on prioritizing profitable growth rather than volume chasing.

In terms of the company’s profitability, gross margins were up 1%, and operating income of $416 million was 9% higher than last year but was actually up 18% organically when adjusting for divestitures. That let the SOI margin expand to 8.5%, up 1% excluding asset sales impacts. By geography, margins were particularly strong in Asia Pacific at 13.1%, with some modest improvement in EMEA.

As for the balance sheet, Goodyear was able to reduce debt by $1.6 billion compared to last year, putting the company’s leverage position in a much healthier position. With deleveraging and lower capex and interest going forward, free cash flow to equity holders is improving.

With the company’s most recent bond deal done at a 6.625% rate on a 5-year note that now trades above par, Goodyear’s cost of capital will likely continue stepping down as it comes time to refinance maturities. As the company’s leverage reduces, I do expect that the company’s B3 rating from Moody’s and B+ rating from S&P to be upgraded.

Improved outlook for 2026

Goodyear’s 2026 outlook feels like a classic case of short-term pain setting up longer-term progress, and I think the market just isn’t giving the company the credit it deserves. Management laid out fairly detailed guidance for Q1’26 but noted that 2026 depends on how quickly markets stabilize. They expect a roughly 10% volume decline in Q1, mainly from weaker U.S. consumer replacement demand as the channel works through high inventories built from late-2025 promotions. January’s severe weather and softer consumer confidence added to the slump.

This softer start is expected to weigh on margins, with about $60 million in unabsorbed overhead and $130 million in tariff and other cost pressures. Offsetting that are around $100 million in savings from the Goodyear Forward program, $85 million from lower raw material costs, and a modest $25 million benefit from improved price and mix. Altogether, the setup makes for a muted first quarter, but I think management’s guidance is realistic. It suggests that most of the inventory destocking pain might be concentrated early, creating some room for volumes to improve as the year goes on.

Looking at 1H’26, Goodyear still faces headwinds from delayed EU tariff decisions and sluggish trends in commercial trucking. The company expects the $175 million full-year tariff impact to be felt mostly in the first two quarters. On the positive side, raw materials should provide roughly $300 million in cost relief, with about two-thirds showing up in the first half, and Goodyear Forward is expected to contribute another $300 million in annual savings. CFO Christina Zamarro hinted that Q2 could mark a turning point, saying sell-in should begin to align more closely with sell-out and that there could be “some pent-up demand” ahead.

For the full year, Goodyear is targeting about 10% organic segment operating income growth from a post-divestiture base of $815 million. The improvement should come from better price and mix in Q2 and Q3, working capital inflows, and lower spending on restructuring, capital, and interest. Management also expects slightly positive free cash flow with potential upside if demand rebounds faster than planned. CEO Mark Stewart reaffirmed that the company is sticking to its Goodyear Forward targets, including the goal of reaching a 10% segment operating income margin.

As a reminder, Goodyear Forward is the company’s multi-year strategy that it launched in 2023 and has been executing to get its earnings back on track. It focuses on streamlining operations, cutting costs, improving its product mix toward higher-end tires. Making the business simpler and more efficient are self-help measures that are meant to drive margin expansion, even in a tougher market.

A lot of their goals aren’t out of reach. If the EU finalizes antidumping duties in the 40-100% range, it could meaningfully reduce low-end import pressure by midyear, boosting Europe’s pricing environment and helping Goodyear’s profitability. Pair that with steady execution on more profitable business, asset sales to deleverage the balance sheet, and a stabilization in consumer demand, and 2026 could shape up as a ‘rebuilding year’ that puts the company on better footing heading into 2027.

Risks to consider

Goodyear still has work to do on its balance sheet. Even after the three asset sales, the whole story assumes they can drive leverage down to roughly 2.0-2.5x and hit a 10% segment operating margin, which leaves very little room for another volume air pocket or a raw material shock without crimping interest coverage again. Any combination of weaker demand, incremental tariffs, or mixed pressure could quickly ‘re‑tighten the screws,’ so to speak, just as they are trying to refinance at better rates and terms.

The other risk is structural and more about whether they can actually grow within their core business. In the tire market, competition is already high, with low-cost imports pressuring pricing and volumes down mid-single digits in recent quarters, and that is before you layer in the capex and R&D needed to stay relevant in electric vehicle fitments. They have clearly made strides with EV-specific products and have won meaningful OE positions in Europe. But if management misjudges the pace of EV adoption or competitors move faster, the payoff on that spend could be underwhelming.

Priced for no growth on valuation

On valuation, given the materially improving earnings profile, I think the market is asleep at the wheel on the story. On just 2025’s improvements, Goodyear is trading at about 13.6x earnings but trades at 6.6x on a forward basis. That figure screams cheap, because it is. Debt has been reduced dramatically, and if the company can get anywhere close to 2023 and 2024 earnings numbers, the stock should have a normalized earnings multiple that’s in the mid-single digits. Even without assuming any growth in price or volume, I think this business trades cheaply.

The market just hasn’t rewarded Goodyear’s execution in the last few years. For the last three years, the stock has been range-bound and as shown below, the company now trades at an EV/EBITDA multiple of 4.3x, at the lower end of the recent historical range (source: Bloomberg).

Compared to peers, Goodyear also trades inexpensively and is priced as if it has no room to further improve its fundamentals. At its current valuation, the company trades at a 1-turn discount on both EV/EBITDA and P/E relative to peers.

Takeaway

Goodyear today looks very different from the company it was just a few years ago. Management has taken meaningful steps to simplify the business, reduce leverage, and focus on higher-margin products, even if those changes temporarily weigh on volumes and headline revenue. While 2026 may begin with softer demand and some near-term pressure from tariffs and inventory adjustments, these are more macro driven and the business trajectory still looks pretty constructive as cost savings, better price and mix, and a stronger balance sheet start to show through. Given how inexpensive the stock looks relative to its improving earnings profile, I think the market is still underestimating the progress Goodyear has made. If the company continues executing on its strategy and demand stabilizes, the stock is going to look fantastically cheap. I’m a buyer of shares at today’s prices.

data.title: EWZS: Why Brazil's Rate-Cut Cycle Could Lift Small Caps Next
data.description:
Thesis

Building on my previous article, I reiterate my bullish stance on the iShares MSCI Brazil Small-Cap ETF (EWZS), supported primarily by the ongoing strong foreign capital inflows into Brazil and the start of a monetary easing cycle. EWZS, in this case, offers exposure to domestic small caps, which historically tend to be more sensitive to falling interest rates and improving economic activity.

The iShares MSCI Brazil Small-Cap ETF, as the name suggests, is one of the easiest ways to get exposure to Brazilian small caps listed on B3, the Brazilian stock exchange. The fund's methodology is based on MSCI classifying companies by capitalization adjusted for free float, where, in the case of EWZS, it takes companies below mid-caps, but excluding micro-caps, with 80 companies making up the ETF.

The index is nonetheless market cap weighted, i.e., larger companies within the Brazilian small-cap space are given greater weight, but even so, the index is relatively diversified on the basis that only 32% of EWZS's assets are held by the top ten holdings, which are rebalanced every quarter.

Many of these companies, such as Lojas Renner (LRENY), Assaí (Sendas Distribuidora) (ASAIY), Multiplan (MLTTY), Inter & Co (INTR), and PagSeguro (PAGS), are little known to foreign investors. Yet, they operate in sectors such as consumer discretionary, real estate, and banking, making them direct proxies for Brazil’s domestic economy, unlike Brazil's main large caps (EWZ), which are mostly commodity-driven.

And that doesn't mean there's a liquidity problem for the ETF. In that respect, the EWZS performs considerably well, with an average daily share volume of 532.84K over the last three months.

In terms of domestic exposure, the fund is quite concentrated on consumer discretionary, representing roughly a quarter of the total holdings, which points towards vulnerability to credit (i.e., exposure to interest rates) and the consumer spending health. This composition helps to explain why the EWZS usually responds with higher volatility to the Selic cycle changes, as it has a standard deviation of 27.36 vs. 23.2 from the EWZ.

Foreign Capital Flows Are Driving Brazil’s Equity Rally

Over the last twelve months, and especially in the first two months of 2026, the strong performance of Brazilian equities, both large, mid, and small caps, is noteworthy, especially versus the S&P 500 (SPY), particularly being driven by an appreciation of the BRL vs. the USD.

What best explains this movement, in my view, is not necessarily a structurally bullish scenario in Brazil's macroeconomy, but rather a flow of foreign investment rotating capital to emerging markets—and with Brazil as one of the primary beneficiaries. Brazil currently has an annual inflation rate of 4.44% (within the tolerance margin of ±1.5% of the 3% target set by the Central Bank of Brazil) and an interest rate of 15% since June 2025. This implies an interest differential of 10.5%, which is extremely high by global standards.

At the same time, in the U.S., interest rates are around ~3.6%, and CPI inflation is at 2.5% per year, which would imply a real interest rate below 1.5%. And given this backdrop, a logical carry trade scenario emerges: investors borrow in USD, and buy assets in BRL.

The first impact of this is felt in fixed-income assets, as the purchase of BRL is applied mostly in Brazilian treasury bonds, and the result is a strengthening of the BRL and a reduction in perceived macro risk. The second impact of this is reflected in equities, where the risk premium is reduced, and valuation becomes attractive for investments.

The headline news points out that in February alone, foreign investors invested R$26.3 billion in Brazilian equities, which is also confirmed in the graph below the significant inflow during the first two full months of 2026. Bearing in mind that capital flows are what have driven up the price of Brazilian equities, it is important to make it clear that this movement is heterogeneous.

This is because when there are large, concentrated flows, foreign investors tend to invest in the most liquid assets, which, in Brazil's case, are large caps. That's why ADRs such as Petrobras (PBR) and Vale (VALE) were some of the biggest beneficiaries of this movement without there necessarily having been any significant change in their fundamentals—except for a few particularities, such as commodity price sensitivity in the case of PBR.

Why EWZS Could Benefit From Brazil’s Next Cycle

Once liquidity and foreign flows hit Brazilian equities hard, I believe the narrative shifts to domestic cyclicality, and I see some strong catalysts that could help trigger a higher alpha for small caps.

The first point I'd make is that small caps tend to be much more sensitive to interest rates because they have higher operating leverage and greater dependence on credit and domestic consumption. And arguably, with an interest rate of 15%, the growth outlook is quite restrictive.

The good news is that, with inflation seemingly stabilized below 5%, the scenario being priced into Brazil today is one of a more dovish monetary policy—analysts predicting cuts of 25 to 50 bps at the next COPOM meeting in March. Although part of this prediction is already embedded in the recent appreciation of EWZS holdings, the signaling of this first cut could set a precedent for others throughout the year, and the fall in the cost of capital is practically immediate, usually leading to a re-rating.

Not to mention that today, 25% of EWZS is made up of consumer discretionary, which is an extremely sensitive segment to improvements in consumption and credit expansion.

The other point is the current valuation of EWZS, especially compared to EWZ. If EWZ today trades at ~13x earnings—above its average over the last five years of ~8x earnings and 2x its book value—the EWZS trades at just 9.2x earnings and 1.1x its book value.

In part, this discount is justified on lower liquidity and, arguably, a higher perception of risk. Still, I can argue that there's a relevant gap between the funds' valuations, and that there is room for a catch-up trade if the capital rotation continues.

Geopolitical Risks Could Disrupt The Trade

Zooming in on the latest developments related to the geopolitical scenario, since the conflict in Iran and the Middle East has escalated, the BRL and Brazilian equities in general have fallen, largely due to the fact that investors tend to run to safer currencies like the USD, reversing the logic of the attractiveness of the EWZ and EWZS.

In the same way, the immediate rise in Brent oil prices (which are already over $80 at the time of writing) will worsen global inflation, which could keep global interest rates higher and eventually strengthen the USD and make the Fed rethink its more dovish stance. This is bad for emerging currencies, as there tends to be an outflow of capital from emerging markets, which would consequently put negative pressure on the EWZS.

But there is also a possible positive scenario if we consider that if the conflict raises oil prices and increases Brazil's terms of trade, the country could receive an influx of commodities. And in this case, these could be variables that sustain the BRL at higher levels and consequently spill over into the appreciation of Brazilian equities, with the flow potentially reaching small caps in a second stage of the trade.

I would also point out that as the presidential elections in Brazil draw closer (scheduled for October), the likelihood of a change in government tends to be largely favorable to equities in general, especially in a more austere government in terms of the fiscal framework. To briefly get into this matter, Brazil's debt-to-GDP ratio, currently sitting at 78.7%, is on an ascending trajectory given the current Lula da Silva administration's inability to post a more material primary surplus. This fiscal imbalance uncertainty is arguably the primary reason for Brazil to carry a structural high-interest rate.

In any case, I still see the next chapter of this fiscal scenario as very blurry, with no obvious definitions and not yet making a price on equities—something that I believe will change from the middle of this year. So I'd expect that the next couple of quarters should be quite a volatile ride for Brazilian small-caps, and the 27.36 standard deviation that the EWZS holds will be able to live up to the risk thesis.

Final Remarks

I remain constructive on Brazilian equities, especially considering that this year's gains have been driven by foreign inflows from a global capital rotation that still seems to be underway. Of course, the escalation of conflicts in the Middle East adds a greater degree of uncertainty and volatility in the short to medium term, which was not necessarily in the script for the EWZS.

Given that (1) foreign flows so far have been mostly concentrated at mid-large caps; (2) a dovish Brazilian Central Bank is the base case scenario; (3) small-cap valuations are still severely discounted, the EWZS seems to me to be an excellent exposure to the macro scenario established today. I anticipate that the falling cost of capital and a less strangled economy with lower interest rates will tend to favor the EWZS more asymmetrically than the EWZ over the course of this year, at least.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Energy, Industrials And Materials: The First Innings Of A Big Market Rotation
data.description:
Introduction

This year, so far, has been quite something.

While I'm writing this, the S&P 500 is basically flat on the year. That’s not a bad thing if we consider that the index returned 16% last year. In 2024, it returned 23%. In the year before that, it returned 24%.

Also, there's the war in Iran.

So yes, if you had any meaningful index exposure or a portfolio with a high correlation to the market, odds are you made a lot of money in the past.

Taking a break after such a blowout performance is only normal, as we have had just one period since the 1980s that saw more than three consecutive strong years. That was ahead of the dot-com bubble, as seen in the chart below from JPMorgan. Think about that.

The thing, however, is that this performance wasn’t evenly distributed. And to be honest, it never is. Since 2015, this has been a very tech-led market, which has concentrated tremendous wealth in that area.

As I have often written, Nvidia (NVDA) alone is worth more than the five smallest S&P 500 sectors combined. That doesn’t mean Nvidia deserves to be sold, yet it shows how much capital in absolute terms is concentrated in some areas.

Now, some of that has started to unwind, and it has happened extremely violently.

As we can see below, year-to-date, energy is up 25%, followed by materials, consumer staples, and industrials. Three of these sectors are cyclical.

Consumer staples are likely among the winners due to their ability to withstand AI disruption. Although I agree with people who make the case that it may be weird to sell a stock like Amazon (AMZN) to buy PepsiCo (PEP), which now has a higher P/E multiple than Amazon, as I have written in the past, I think it’s a different story due to a lot of capital rushing into safety.

And, to get back to my main point, because there is so much capital in certain areas, even a slight rotation can have a violent impact on smaller sectors and stocks. That’s what we are witnessing right now.

In this article, I’ll update you on my rotation thesis, explain what this means for the S&P 500, and why I expect to generate substantial alpha by focusing on the areas where capital is “forced” to go.

So, let’s get to it!

We’re In The Early Innings At Best

While I'm writing this, the conflict in Iran is still ongoing. Given the fast-moving headlines, the situation may be different by the time you’re reading this. And, to be honest, that doesn’t matter to my thesis, as I do not write on energy and similar sectors because of the war.

I'm a long-term investor. I care about the big picture.

One great “big picture chart” from Macro/Micro can be seen below. It shows the combined weighting of industrials, materials, and energy. Right now, these are three of the four biggest year-to-date gainers in the S&P 500. They are also all capex-intensive, cyclical, and hard to disrupt.

Although it's hard to see in the chart above, right now, these three sectors have a combined weighting of roughly 14% in the S&P 500. In November, that number was just 12%. In 2008, it was 29%. In 1994, it was 31%.

These comparisons are always a bit odd, as the world was different in 1994 and 2008. Since then, we've had the emerging Mag-7 group of stocks, the birth of AI, and a whole bunch of other secular growth drivers like software, cloud computing, and other applications that can be grown with very limited capex and efforts (excluding AI, of course).

The past 10 years were bad for value investors, at least on a relative basis to the S&P 500, as this market-weighted index simply let its winners run and build an index that was led by some of the most innovative companies on earth. Right now, these are still leading the market.

Good for them. And I mean that. I’m not a hater. I have said it before and say it again: I like players like Amazon and Alphabet (GOOGL) and would like to buy them in the quarters ahead.

What’s so critical to understand is that we are seeing a paradigm shift in the economy and markets. As different as 2000-2008 may have been from 2015–2021 (to name two highly different market environments), that’s how different I think 2026 and beyond will be as well.

  • For the first time since 2021, cyclical economic growth is improving, which gives investors a real alternative to diversify, as big tech isn’t the only game in town anymore. Obviously, there were other winners, but the growth problem is a major reason why capital has become so concentrated.
  • AI has turned big tech into capex-intensive companies that are basically at the mercy of bottlenecks like power demand. As Elon Musk said last year, companies will run into power shortages, which means companies will order GPUs and other chips that they cannot make money from, as there’s not enough power.
  • AI disruption has massively altered the environment. Software, for example, which accounts for more than 25% of the weighting of the tech sector, has become a minefield of uncertainty. In a market where uncertainty has gone through the roof, the market is simply unwilling to apply a high multiple to the industries that performed so well in the past. It’s basically one big re-rating.
  • Inflation is much stickier, fueled by unusual factors like the elevated federal deficit, AI bottlenecks, deglobalization, and efforts to kickstart the economy (think about adding liquidity to get the housing market going).

Now, let me give you more data.

As many of you know, I use leading indicators like the ISM Manufacturing Index to assess the cyclical part of the economy. Last year, my belief that we would see upside momentum made me invest a lot of capital into energy and industrials. Earlier this year, we finally got evidence of improvements, as the ISM Manufacturing Index soared above 50, which is the neutral line between expansion and contraction.

In February, that number was confirmed, as the index remained at 52.4. While that may be 0.2 points below the January level, it’s perfectly normal for a volatile index to move around a bit.

Moreover, we’re seeing a spike in prices paid, which is another good indicator for sticky inflation, as the surge in commodity prices (before oil took off) has already impacted the bills that industrial companies get from their suppliers.

But wait. There’s more!

The Chicago Business Barometer (the Chicago version of the ISM Index) kept rising. It’s now at 57.7, which is the second-positive (above 50) reading after spending 25 months below 50. That’s the rotation I have been talking about for so long. Even more important, this was fully supported by higher production, higher orders, and higher deliveries.

I generalize a lot, but when dealing with big-picture developments, that makes sense.

Although the Iran situation is making things highly uncertain, these developments tell investors that they finally have a green light to invest in cyclical assets again.

Here’s what Craig Fuller said (in light of the ISM surge), one of the greatest experts in transportation, if you ask me:

Flatbed trucking continues to break all-time records for rejections, as trucking capacity evaporates for heavy industry due to the onslaught of manufacturing activity.

Very important: Housing is not a factor currently, which tells us how insane of a recovery in manufacturing that is underway. - Craig Fuller on X

And these developments are moving quickly, as even net zero has stalled.

Back in 2020/2021, “net zero” really started to gain momentum as everyone was talking about decarbonization, investments in hydrogen, EV mobility, solar, and more. While these are all future-proof areas, net zero, as a plan, is failing, as Javier Blas wrote in a recent opinion piece.

The reason is simple, which is that demand for fossil fuels is way stronger than initially expected. And, to be honest, I think we’ll see demand grow even faster:

But here we are in 2026 with global demand for oil, natural gas and coal at an all-time high and likely to climb further, a far cry from the trajectory required to achieve net zero. When rich nations got together last week to debate their energy policy, ministers were more worried about the security of energy supplies — and prices. Climate change remains important, but no longer dominates the agenda. Fatih Birol, the head of the International Energy Agency and a longtime cheerleader of the net-zero movement, was paradigmatic of the shift. In his opening speech at the IEA ministerial meeting last week in Paris, he mentioned “energy security” eight times; “affordability” got four citations; “climate,” two. And what about “net zero”? Well, ahem, zero. - Bloomberg

Here’s a chart showing Newcastle coal futures (one of the biggest markets for thermal coal), which tells us that despite recent cyclical weakness, coal prices never made it to pre-pandemic lows:

This entire energy rotation is further boosted by power-hungry data centers as the market has finally figured out that it doesn’t matter how much capital hyperscalers have. They cannot print power. They all need power. That’s why I'm even more bullish on oil and gas producers and midstream companies (the pipeline owners) than I already was.

As dramatic as this may sound, this sector benefits from forced capital spending, as it doesn’t matter what the state of the economy is, as hyperscalers need to spend billions on power, often even “behind the meter.” This includes midstream companies like ONEOK (OKE). This is what the Bear Traps Report wrote on that when they brought up just how bullish this area has become:

[...] hyperscalers are scrambling for gigawatts that renewables can’t deliver fast enough, so nat gas-fired plants (and behind-the-meter direct connects) become the default, and ONEOK’s 60k-mile footprint plus new dedicated spurs to 500MW+ data centers are the toll road collecting fees on every molecule. Management is already fielding direct asks from AI firms (“how can you help us power this?”), volumes are set to compound on top of Permian/Mid-Con growth and LNG pull, while $870M+ synergies from the latest deals drop straight to the bottom line [...] - The Bear Traps Report (Paywalled Report)

Even oil producers like Diamondback Energy (FANG) are now in talks to establish power production on their land:

And we're making progress on bringing data centers onto our surface position. I think as you think about Diamondback specifically, the biggest benefit here is our ability to structure a power purchase agreement that provides for material uplift to nat gas pricing. So just another creative tool in the toolbox for us as we are thinking about improving natural gas realizations, which we obviously highlighted in the deck and Kaes alluded to earlier. - FANG 4Q25 Earnings Call

These developments matter so much because, on top of rising demand for energy due to the cyclical upswing (among other factors), the market will get a reason to re-rate energy due to new tailwinds like direct deals with power producers and AI bottleneck benefits.

The chart below shows the energy-to-S&P 500 ratio. In just two months, it erased roughly 1.5 years of underperformance. In other words, if you had bought both XLE and SPY (or any S&P 500 index fund) in August 2024, you would now have the same return on both.

This is what the chart looks like when we zoom out:

Meanwhile, the industrials-to-S&P 500 ratio just hit its highest levels since 2020, as it took just three months to erase five years of underperformance:

Essentially, we’re seeing re-ratings across the board.

The market leaders of the past decades are mostly seeing valuation re-ratings to the downside to incorporate new risks, while the cyclical laggards are seeing positive re-ratings.

And, as I expect all of these factors to last, I think the rotation thesis remains in terrific shape.

The “Quick and Dirty” Playbook

There are many ways to play this. One of them is by simply buying the S&P 500 and ignoring everything else. On a very long-term basis, that’s certainly a great way to play this.

However, as the rotation continues to get confirmation from capital flows, leading economic indicators, and other developments in areas like AI, I believe this is a terrific environment to somewhat avoid what worked well in the past and make some changes to improve the risk/reward of a simple investment in the S&P 500.

That’s why I stick to my bullish view on cyclical value, including energy (power), industrials (machinery and hardware), and basic materials (chemicals). To me, that’s the trifecta to generate alpha in the years ahead.

Needless to say, it won’t be an easy ride, as volatility is likely here to stay. Especially after the strong performance of these assets in the first two months of this year, I would not be surprised if we were to see some weakness as traders take profits and investors like me prepare to buy more on weakness.

So, whatever you do, I think complementing the S&P 500 (or any portfolio) with investments in these areas is a great way to improve the risk/reward.

To summarize:

  • I think the S&P 500 will do well, yet show returns that are likely substantially below prior-year levels.
  • I think current market leaders will remain market leaders.
  • After a strong January/February performance, I expect a short counter-rotation that offers new buying opportunities.

In general, I believe we’re in a true regime change that will last years and push weightings back to multi-year levels. As we have seen what the first innings of this move have done to the market, I'm extremely upbeat about what’s likely to come.

And, as I already summarized this piece, I can keep the takeaway short today.

Takeaway

I don’t think we’re in a broken market. What we are dealing with is a major rotation.

While the S&P 500 will likely be fine, alpha is shifting to energy, industrials, and materials. So far, the data confirms this, as capital has started to move (with a huge effect), AI is accelerating “real economy” capital flows, and investors have figured out that the factors that supported big tech in 2015-2021 are now creating a new environment for a whole different set of stocks.

As a result, I'm looking to expand my investments in these areas, and I will keep you up to date on more specific investments in the days and weeks ahead.

Risks to My Thesis

An economic recession is likely the biggest risk, as it could end the bullish thesis for cyclical stocks. As big tech is weakened, I expect that a potential recession could push capital into safety, including bonds and gold.
